original_abstract: The catalytic performance of supported metal nanoparticles is fundamentally governed by the dynamic evolution of their active sites under reaction conditions. In this work, we report a light-induced strategy to manipulate the structural and compositional restructuring of Cu species on a TiO₂ support during low-temperature methanol steam reforming (MSR). We observe that under dark conditions, the catalyst undergoes rapid thermal deactivation as the active metallic Cu sites are oxidatively restructured into inoperative Cu₂O by strongly bound *CH₃O intermediates. However, the introduction of UV light triggers a restorative transformation, where photo-excited charge carriers from the TiO₂ support reduce the Cu₂O back to the active metallic state. This in situ structural regulation results in a dramatic enhancement of both catalytic activity and long-term stability. Under low-intensity solar irradiation, the optimized Cu/TiO₂ catalyst achieves an H₂ production rate of 1724.1 μmol g⁻¹ min⁻¹, surpassing most reported noble-metal-based and conventional thermocatalytic systems. Combined operando characterizations and DFT-based molecular dynamics simulations provide atomistic insights into the mechanism of this charge-carrier-mediated restructuring. These findings highlight the potential of utilizing light–matter–reactant interactions to achieve real-time functionalization and stabilization of non-noble-metal catalysts for sustainable energy applications.
humanized_abstract: The activity and stability of catalysts are of prime interest in many catalytic reactions. The catalytic performance of supported metal nanoparticles is thought to be governed by the formation and dynamics of the active sites during catalytic reactions. Herein, we present a novel light-activated method for controlling the structural evolution and compositional adjustment of Cu species on TiO2 during low-temperature MSR. Our results indicate that under dark conditions, the active Cu species were found to be oxidized into inactive Cu2O by *CH3O species that are strongly adsorbed on the Cu species, leading to a fast thermal deactivation of the catalyst. Interestingly, when UV light was added into the reaction system, the activity of the catalyst was successfully restored by exciting the charge carriers on the TiO2 support that reduced Cu2O into active Cu species. Low intensity solar light (35 mW cm−2) of 1724.1 μmol g−1 min−1 for H2 production, which was much higher than that of the noble metal catalysts such as Ru/C (1241 μmol g−1 min−1) and Pd/C (1472 μmol g−1 min−1) and most thermocatalytic catalysts (Table S6). Operando-XAS and -Raman combined with DFT simulations reveals the unique light-induced reduction of Cu2O into active Cu species through light–matter–reactant interactions at the catalyst surface.
